http://web.archive.org/web/20240711183700im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2010/12/05/nyregion/05-LAPTOPISTAN/05-LAPTOPISTAN-jumbo.jpg?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 1024w" sizes="((min-width: 600px) and (max-width: 1004px)) 84vw, (min-width: 1005px) 60vw, 100vw" decoding="async" width="600" height="315"/></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-ImageCaption" class="css-ktho12 e3rygrp0"><span class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">GROSS DOMESTIC PRODUCT</strong> It may not look like much goes on in the laptop brigade at Atlas Café in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, but the room is a hive of entrepreneurial and creative energy.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div><div data-testid="byline-timestamp" class="css-xt80pu eakwutd0"><div class="css-103l8m3"><div class="css-1e2jphy epjyd6m1"><div class="css-233int epjyd6m0"><p class="css-4anu6l e1jsehar1"><span class="byline-prefix">By </span><span class="css-1baulvz last-byline" itemprop="name">David Sax</span></p></div></div><div data-testid="reading-time-module" class="css-8atqhb"><ul class="css-1cgskve epjyd6m3"><li class="css-ccw2r3 epjyd6m2"><time class="css-1z1nqv e16638kd0" datetime="2010-12-03T18:45:34-05:00">Dec. 3, 2010</time></li></ul></div></div></div></header><section name="articleBody" class="meteredContent css-1r7ky0e"><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">JUST after 4 o’clock on a Wednesday afternoon, as a dozen people clicked away on their laptops at the Atlas Café in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, half of a tree broke off without warning less than a block away. It crashed into the middle of Havemeyer Street, crushing a parked car, setting off alarms and blocking the street. A deafening chorus of horns rose outside Atlas’s window as traffic halted. An 18-wheeler executed a sketchy 10-point turn in the middle of a crowded intersection before a pair of fire trucks made their way through the traffic jam in a blaze of red. Chain saws roared, sawdust flew and the horns built to a peak. It was New York urban pandemonium at its finest.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0"><a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240711183700/http://www.atlas-cafe.com/" title="The cafe’s Web site." rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Inside the warm confines of Atlas</a>, separated from the chaos by only a thin wall of glass, not a soul stirred. A quiet mention was made of the falling tree, a few heads rose for a second, and then, just as quickly, they ducked back down. They all returned to whatever was on the other side of their glowing, partly eaten apples. On a day when the cafe Internet connection had already been down for four hours, and the toilet had been blocked for even longer, I thought I had seen these worker bees pushed to their limit. But I had underestimated them. Nothing could stir these people. They were not in New York; they were citizens of Laptopistan.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">I was an interloper among them, an anthropologist of sorts, sent to untangle their odd society, to understand their mores and unwritten rules. How did the natives interact? How did the government function? What was the economy like in this land of bottomless cups and table hoggers? And what, oh what, were they all writing?</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">I was, admittedly, a profoundly skeptical observer. Though I had been a freelance writer for the last eight years, I had always worked at home, clad in pajamas and brewing my own fuel rather than paying $3 for someone to make pretty designs in my caffeinated foam. Whenever my wife suggested that I get out of the house, maybe take my laptop to a cafe, I shot back: “Real freelancers don’t work in coffee shops. It’s just unemployed hipsters and their unpublished novels, or screenplays, or Facebook stati.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">My disdain for the coffee shop writing scene only grew this June, when I met a friend at the Red Horse Cafe in Park Slope. I had previously been there only on weekends, when it was filled with couples sharing the Sunday paper and parents wiping brownie residue off their toddlers. That weekday afternoon it was a place transformed. Gone were the newspapers and the strollers. Laptops had colonized every flat surface. No one uttered a word; people just stared into screens, expressionless. It felt like that moment in a horror movie when the innocent couple stumbles into a house filled with hibernating zombies, and they listen, in terror, as the floorboard creaks.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">My friend and I ordered our coffee, and began to discuss whatever it was we were there to discuss. Within seconds of opening my mouth, I felt a change in the room. Eyes rose from their screens and landed on us — the zombies were awake, and they weren’t happy. One of them passed our table on the way to the washroom and let loose what can only be described as a snicker. We downed our coffee and beat a quick retreat. “To hell with those laptop-wielding fundamentalists,” I said.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">So what was I doing in Laptopistan? I moved from New York to Toronto in September, but had come back to the city for a week and was sleeping on a friend’s couch. I needed a place to work. Someone suggested Atlas. I swallowed my skepticism and got my passport ready.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">SET on the corner of Havemeyer and Grand Streets, and flooded with light from two walls of windows, Atlas Café, which opened in 2003, looks like a combination of worn trattoria and late 1990s Seattle coffeehouse. The name reflects its wall-sized map of the world (there are also a mobile of hanging globes, and flourishes of décor inspired by someone’s travel to the Far East). The soundtrack is a mix of old country and folk (Dylan, Willy, Cohen and Cash), classical, bebop and French ballads.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">A makeshift milk station sits in the center of the 750-square-foot room. To the left is a long bar of dark stained rustic wood, where people order food and drink (proper espressos for $1.50, excellent panini, salads and deadly little chocolate-filled Italian doughnuts, $3), as well as the windowless, orange washroom. There are three stools at the bar, and beside them a table of reclaimed timber pressed against a cozy window bench. According to two signs, this little corner, perhaps one-sixth of the cafe, is designated a laptop-free zone.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Everything beyond is Laptopistan: two rows of old church pews formed into an elongated L, each fronted by small wooden tables and chairs. Scattered along the pews are tattered blue tubular pillows, for lumbar support. Windows run along the walls, flooding the computer zone with natural light during the day, creating a fishbowl effect for those inside looking out, and for passers-by, who frequently stop and stare at the tableau.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Entering Laptopistan is a task in itself. The floor presents an obstacle course of power cables snaking their way around coffee cups, over chairs, and around table legs, eventually finding a home in one of two power strips that look as though they came from a Soviet coal refinery. Whenever a plug is inserted, the outlet sparks, and certain movements can cause all the plugs on a given strip to simultaneously eject.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">A quick glance around shows I have brought my laptop, a month-old MacBook Pro, to its nesting ground. Diversity here means the odd white MacBook or old black PowerBook scattered amid the silver MacBook Pros. Throughout the week I will see only a handful of PCs, each looking sadly out of place, like they have arrived at a black-tie affair in a corduroy blazer.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">I worked for a few hours, and quickly learned the principal laws of Laptopistan:</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Silence Is Golden. There is no prohibition on talking, of course, but, as one Atlas regular of several years, Joelle Hann, explained, “there’s almost a code that people aren’t going to talk loud.” When people’s phones ring, they run outside as fast as possible to take the call.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“If someone’s on Skype or having a conversation, people make an effort to chill out their conversations with looks,” said Ms. Hann, a yoga teacher and a freelance journalist and textbook editor. “When they don’t stop, you can feel the tension.” Shushing conversations is equally verboten. “No one wants to be the librarian,” Ms. Hann added.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Respect Personal Space. While any open seat is technically available, it is forbidden to set up your computer on a table with a computer already on it; doubling up is allowed only when all tables are taken. At the same time, people bounce from table to table throughout the day, chasing the sun, the shade or their own feng shui.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Mind Your Neighborhood. When you get up to take a call, get coffee or use the washroom, you need only to look at a neighbor, make eye contact, look back at your computer and nod. The deal is sealed without a word: You watch my MacBook and I’ll watch yours.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="sizeMedium layoutHorizontal css-malmms ewenx681"><a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240711183700/https://www.nytimes.com/slideshow/2010/12/05/nyregion/05LAPTOPISTAN.html"><div class="css-5nx6oe"><h2 class="css-18ca7pz">Coffee, Tea and Laptops</h2><div class="css-chloe3"><p class="css-el7lf5"></p><p class="css-el7lf5">9 Photos</p><p class="css-el7lf5">View Slide Show <span class="css-t4350i">›</span></p></div></div><div class="css-79elbk"><div class="css-1izowsy"></div><div data-testid="imageblock-wrapper"><figure class="sizeMedium layoutHorizontal ewenx680 css-i2hvst" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xb94ky" data-testid="imageContainer-children-Image"><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.9036458333333px"></div></div></div><figcaption data-testid="photoviewer-children-ImageCaption" class="css-ktho12 e3rygrp0"></figcaption></figure></div></div></a><div class="css-ktho12 efyb8hk0"><span class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times</span></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">MOST Laptopistanis — Laptopistanites? Laptopistanians? — at Atlas are in their 20s, 30s or early 40s, split evenly between men and women. The dress is casual, with both sexes wearing T-shirts, sweaters and jeans, though a few women seem dressed for “work” with button-down blouses, blazers, even a dress or two. Most Laptopistanis work alone, though occasionally I spotted a group collaborating at a corner table. Socially, Laptopistan is a conservative society; outward displays of emotion are frowned upon. Most people hide behind their screens.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Aaron Tugendhaft is the exception. He appears at Atlas every morning for a few hours, tie askew, black coffee at his side, some heady-looking book in front of him. Mr. Tugendhaft, who is an adjunct professor of religion at New York University and the editor of a small custom press, is one of the only Atlas regulars I observed sans laptop.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“I’ve made friends with people because I’m the only guy without a computer,” he told me, quietly, one morning. “A book can be a conversation starter.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Mr. Tugendhaft has been coming to Atlas nearly every day for three and a half years, but there are many Laptopistanis he has never spoken to. (“Some of them are in the room right now,” he confided in a low voice, eyeing a woman in a jean jacket two tables over.) He has dated fellow Laptopistanis, but not anymore, preferring to keep romance out of the workplace. People tend to keep to themselves, he said, until something breaks the routine: an argument between lovers, news of a subway breakdown, or, most often, some sort of interaction around the power strips.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Power is power,” Mr. Tugendhaft said.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Mallory Roberts, a longhaired freelance astrophysicist, said that he had met two girlfriends (now exes), and his current roommate, via power-strip negotiations. “Three months ago Courtney was looking for a plug and we got to talking,” he said of the roommate, with whom he is now working on a documentary about a giant telescope in Puerto Rico. “It turns out we went to the same university, and she needed a place to stay.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“There’s a recognition here,” he added, “that people come to a cafe to not be alone.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">THE cost of living in Laptopistan, at least for my personal intake of tea, bagels, sandwiches and salad, averaged $12 a day. Other cafes popular with freelancers charge for wireless Internet access and even table time, but the owners of the Atlas, Enrico Lorenzetti and Luca Tesconi, refuse to. Observing the gross daily consumption in Laptopistan, where people seem to nurse a cup of coffee and a cookie for hours, I could not imagine how the two managed to stay in business. But they said the laptops were a stealth economic engine.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">While the people behind the screens spent a paltry $6 to $10 per day, their true value is as a draw for more profitable takeout customers, Mr. Lorenzetti said. From the moment the door opens at 7 a.m. until it closes at 9 p.m., the place is buzzing, a productive society, visible from the street through wraparound windows. “People come in to buy food and coffee to go, because they see a full crowd,” he said. “They think ‘Hey, this place must be good if I can’t even get a table.’ ”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">My long-held notion that Laptopistan’s citizens were just sitting around e-mailing other writers in other cafes around the world dissipated as I got to know the MacBook Pro owners around me. Sure, there were aspiring screenwriters, novelists and people updating Twitter, but there was also Gauri Nanda, a product designer from Detroit who created<a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240711183700/http://www.nandahome.com/products/clocky/" title="About Clocky." rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank"> Clocky</a>, the alarm clock on wheels that’s featured at the MoMA store and sold worldwide. There was Billy Schultz, a corporate human resources consultant crunching numbers for spreadsheets in PowerPoint and Excel (on a Lenovo PC, no less), and Meredith Sadin, working on her doctorate in American politics at Princeton.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Laptopistan’s is an entrepreneurial economy, driven by solitary thinkers. <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20240711183700/http://www.aszurebarton.com/" title="The choreographer’s Web site." rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Aszure Barton</a>, a choreographer from Alberta, was working with colleagues to prepare for her contemporary dance show called BUSK, which will debut Dec. 17 at the Jerome Robbins Theater. Robert Olinger runs a biotech startup that is getting silkworms to make spider silk at commercial scale, designs online education programs for the New York City Department of Education, and directs theater projects with Russian artists. In just a few days I met architects and event planners, database designers, classical musicians, film editors and app developers, every facet of the creative economy working under one roof, not so much together as in tandem.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Here, people have large ambitions,” Mr. Olinger said. “Some have resources, some don’t. They don’t have career plans mapped out, but they have a career in mind. They’re not looking at a particular ladder to climb, they’re looking at a mountain to conquer.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">WHEN that tree fell that Wednesday afternoon, I was one of the only people who walked over to check it out. I knew I was becoming one of them when a voice in my head said: “O.K., time to get back to work.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">At home, the slightest change in light is enough of an excuse to get up, walk around, clip my nails or head into the kitchen. Though home offices seem like the perfect work environment, their unrestricted silence, uninterrupted solitude and creature comforts breed distraction. In Laptopistan, I focused with intense precision, sitting motionless for hours at a time. At home, when the Internet goes down, I feel professionally castrated, and usually retire to the sofa until the connection is restored. When the wireless network at Atlas seized up two afternoons in a row, I just switched to off-line tasks without breaking stride. No one else seemed fazed, either. If anything, the place got more crowded. Laptopistan provides structure, and freelancers, like children, secretly crave structure. You come to work, for two or four or eight hours, and you take comfort in the knowledge that everyone else is there to work as well. There’s a silent social pressure to it all.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“It almost sounds sick,” said Selena Ross, a fellow Canadian freelance journalist I sat next to one day, “but the fact that people are watching me do my work helps me be what they expect me to be. It’s like working exhibitionism.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Back home in Toronto, with my ergonomically correct chair, spacious desk and dedicated Internet connection, I pulled up my notes from the journey to Laptopistan and tried to write. Within 10 minutes, I was lost in Facebook and watching old “Soul Train” clips on YouTube.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">So I unplugged my laptop, traded my sweat pants for jeans and walked two blocks to the nearest coffee shop. There was some country music playing at a comfortable volume, and the familiar sight of cords along the floor. I took a seat between a guy working on an identical MacBook Pro and a woman drawing in a journal, and I worked like I was back in Atlas: productively, contentedly, fueled by a steady diet of Earl Grey tea, an economically acceptable quantity of cookies, and that social pressure I was craving.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">After an hour or so, the guy next to me got up to go to the washroom, turned toward his laptop and looked at me.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">With a nod, I completed the transaction. When he walked away, I smiled. It was not a big smile; most likely it was barely visible to anyone looking beyond my screen. Precisely the secret smile that citizens of Laptopistan allow themselves when no one’s looking.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div></section><div class="bottom-of-article"><div class="css-5dm9xx"><div class="css-acwcvw"><strong class="css-1vg6q84">A correction was made on</strong> <div class="css-cz9nt9 e16638kd1">Dec. 12, 2010</div>: <p class="css-o5w3xl evys1bk0">An article last Sunday about people who sit in cafes and use laptops misspelled the given name of a singer whose music is played in one cafe, the Atlas in Williamsburg. He is Willie Nelson, not Willy.</p></div><p>How we handle corrections</p></div><div class="css-lojhqv">A version of this article appears in print on <span class="css-1dmwf73"> </span>, Section <!-- -->MB<!-- -->, Page <!-- -->1<!-- --> of the New York edition<!-- --> with the headline: <!-- -->Destination: LAPTOPISTAN<span>. <a DOMESTIC PRODUCT\u003C\u002Fstrong\u003E It may not look like much goes on in the laptop brigade at Atlas Café in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, but the room is a hive of entrepreneurial and creative energy.","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F347ce198-1021-5e81-807a-900695bcce87","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05-LAPTOPISTAN.html"},"size":"MEDIUM"},"subhead":null,"timestampBlock":{"__typename":"TimestampBlock","align":"DEFAULT","showUpdatedTimestamp":null,"timestamp":"2010-12-03T23:45:34.000Z"}},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"JUST after 4 o’clock on a Wednesday afternoon, as a dozen people clicked away on their laptops at the Atlas Café in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, half of a tree broke off without warning less than a block away. It crashed into the middle of Havemeyer Street, crushing a parked car, setting off alarms and blocking the street. A deafening chorus of horns rose outside Atlas’s window as traffic halted. An 18-wheeler executed a sketchy 10-point turn in the middle of a crowded intersection before a pair of fire trucks made their way through the traffic jam in a blaze of red. Chain saws roared, sawdust flew and the horns built to a peak. It was New York urban pandemonium at its finest."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":0},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","title":"The cafe’s Web site.","uri":null,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.atlas-cafe.com\u002F"}],"text":"Inside the warm confines of Atlas"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":", separated from the chaos by only a thin wall of glass, not a soul stirred. A quiet mention was made of the falling tree, a few heads rose for a second, and then, just as quickly, they ducked back down. They all returned to whatever was on the other side of their glowing, partly eaten apples. On a day when the cafe Internet connection had already been down for four hours, and the toilet had been blocked for even longer, I thought I had seen these worker bees pushed to their limit. But I had underestimated them. Nothing could stir these people. They were not in New York; they were citizens of Laptopistan."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":1},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"I was an interloper among them, an anthropologist of sorts, sent to untangle their odd society, to understand their mores and unwritten rules. How did the natives interact? How did the government function? What was the economy like in this land of bottomless cups and table hoggers? And what, oh what, were they all writing?"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":2},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"I was, admittedly, a profoundly skeptical observer. Though I had been a freelance writer for the last eight years, I had always worked at home, clad in pajamas and brewing my own fuel rather than paying $3 for someone to make pretty designs in my caffeinated foam. Whenever my wife suggested that I get out of the house, maybe take my laptop to a cafe, I shot back: “Real freelancers don’t work in coffee shops. It’s just unemployed hipsters and their unpublished novels, or screenplays, or Facebook stati.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":3},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"My disdain for the coffee shop writing scene only grew this June, when I met a friend at the Red Horse Cafe in Park Slope. I had previously been there only on weekends, when it was filled with couples sharing the Sunday paper and parents wiping brownie residue off their toddlers. That weekday afternoon it was a place transformed. Gone were the newspapers and the strollers. Laptops had colonized every flat surface. No one uttered a word; people just stared into screens, expressionless. It felt like that moment in a horror movie when the innocent couple stumbles into a house filled with hibernating zombies, and they listen, in terror, as the floorboard creaks."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":4},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"My friend and I ordered our coffee, and began to discuss whatever it was we were there to discuss. Within seconds of opening my mouth, I felt a change in the room. Eyes rose from their screens and landed on us — the zombies were awake, and they weren’t happy. One of them passed our table on the way to the washroom and let loose what can only be described as a snicker. We downed our coffee and beat a quick retreat. “To hell with those laptop-wielding fundamentalists,” I said."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":5},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"So what was I doing in Laptopistan? I moved from New York to Toronto in September, but had come back to the city for a week and was sleeping on a friend’s couch. I needed a place to work. Someone suggested Atlas. I swallowed my skepticism and got my passport ready."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":6},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"SET on the corner of Havemeyer and Grand Streets, and flooded with light from two walls of windows, Atlas Café, which opened in 2003, looks like a combination of worn trattoria and late 1990s Seattle coffeehouse. The name reflects its wall-sized map of the world (there are also a mobile of hanging globes, and flourishes of décor inspired by someone’s travel to the Far East). The soundtrack is a mix of old country and folk (Dylan, Willy, Cohen and Cash), classical, bebop and French ballads."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":7},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"A makeshift milk station sits in the center of the 750-square-foot room. To the left is a long bar of dark stained rustic wood, where people order food and drink (proper espressos for $1.50, excellent panini, salads and deadly little chocolate-filled Italian doughnuts, $3), as well as the windowless, orange washroom. There are three stools at the bar, and beside them a table of reclaimed timber pressed against a cozy window bench. According to two signs, this little corner, perhaps one-sixth of the cafe, is designated a laptop-free zone."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":8},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Everything beyond is Laptopistan: two rows of old church pews formed into an elongated L, each fronted by small wooden tables and chairs. Scattered along the pews are tattered blue tubular pillows, for lumbar support. Windows run along the walls, flooding the computer zone with natural light during the day, creating a fishbowl effect for those inside looking out, and for passers-by, who frequently stop and stare at the tableau."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":9},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Entering Laptopistan is a task in itself. The floor presents an obstacle course of power cables snaking their way around coffee cups, over chairs, and around table legs, eventually finding a home in one of two power strips that look as though they came from a Soviet coal refinery. Whenever a plug is inserted, the outlet sparks, and certain movements can cause all the plugs on a given strip to simultaneously eject."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":10},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"A quick glance around shows I have brought my laptop, a month-old MacBook Pro, to its nesting ground. Diversity here means the odd white MacBook or old black PowerBook scattered amid the silver MacBook Pros. Throughout the week I will see only a handful of PCs, each looking sadly out of place, like they have arrived at a black-tie affair in a corduroy blazer."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":11},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"I worked for a few hours, and quickly learned the principal laws of Laptopistan:"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Silence Is Golden. There is no prohibition on talking, of course, but, as one Atlas regular of several years, Joelle Hann, explained, “there’s almost a code that people aren’t going to talk loud.” When people’s phones ring, they run outside as fast as possible to take the call."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":12},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“If someone’s on Skype or having a conversation, people make an effort to chill out their conversations with looks,” said Ms. Hann, a yoga teacher and a freelance journalist and textbook editor. “When they don’t stop, you can feel the tension.” Shushing conversations is equally verboten. “No one wants to be the librarian,” Ms. Hann added."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":13},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Respect Personal Space. While any open seat is technically available, it is forbidden to set up your computer on a table with a computer already on it; doubling up is allowed only when all tables are taken. At the same time, people bounce from table to table throughout the day, chasing the sun, the shade or their own feng shui."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":14},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Mind Your Neighborhood. When you get up to take a call, get coffee or use the washroom, you need only to look at a neighbor, make eye contact, look back at your computer and nod. The deal is sealed without a word: You watch my MacBook and I’ll watch yours."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":true,"index":15},{"__typename":"SlideshowBlock","media":{"__typename":"Slideshow","displayProperties":{"__typename":"CreativeWorkDisplayProperties","template":"600px Slideshow"},"headline":{"__typename":"CreativeWorkHeadline","default":"Coffee, Tea and Laptops"},"id":"U2xpZGVzaG93Om55dDovL3NsaWRlc2hvdy8zNTRlYmM1Mi1mNTgyLTU0YmYtODMzYi1iNjg1MGFkM2RlYzc=","promotionalMedia":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times","id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMTQ1ZmM3MjEtNDFlOC01NDhjLWIzY2UtNDA2NGI1NGY4MjNl","slideshowCrops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":683,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W-jumbo.jpg","width":1024}]}],"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W.html"},"slides":[{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Atlas Caf&#233;, in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, laptop jockeys take up most of the seats, deep in their own world and creative economy.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York on the corner of Havemeyer and Grand Streets, the cafe is part 1990s Seattle coffee house, part worn trattoria.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York conversation does occur, it may be a negotiation over a power strip. People come to work, not to socialize.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times","crops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"THREE_BY_TWO","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":126,"name":"thumbWide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L-thumbWide.jpg","width":190}]},{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"MASTER","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":683,"name":"jumbo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L-jumbo.jpg","width":1024},{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":400,"name":"slide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-NM0L-slide.jpg","width":600}]}],"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvYTkyZjU1MzEtYTE5ZS01MTI4LWFjYzktOGU4ZGNlZTJjNGM3","summary":"","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002Fa92f5531-a19e-5128-acc9-8e8dcee2c4c7"},"legacyHtmlCaption":"\u003Cp\u003EDavid Sax, freelance journalist, took his critical eye to the Atlas to learn about the ways of Laptopistan. One obvious trait: Macintosh computers are the favorite.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times","crops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"THREE_BY_TWO","renditions":[]},{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"MASTER","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":683,"name":"jumbo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-FPHJ\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-FPHJ-jumbo.jpg","width":1024},{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":400,"name":"slide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-FPHJ\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-FPHJ-slide.jpg","width":600}]}],"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvN2NjMjI4NDEtOGVlMi01OTg3LWJkZWQtMDQ2ZjkwYzYxZjMx","summary":"","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F7cc22841-8ee2-5987-bded-046f90c61f31"},"legacyHtmlCaption":"\u003Cp\u003EAn owner of the Atlas, Luca Tesconi. He and the co-owner of the cafe say they don't mind the Laptopistanis hanging out. In fact, they say the full room makes the place look popular.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times","crops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"THREE_BY_TWO","renditions":[]},{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"MASTER","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":683,"name":"jumbo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-QKX5\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-QKX5-jumbo.jpg","width":1024},{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":400,"name":"slide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-QKX5\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-QKX5-slide.jpg","width":600}]}],"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMWZkZTM5OTItMmU5OC01NjU1LWJlZjAtNTAxMTBkMjI1MjBh","summary":"","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F1fde3992-2e98-5655-bef0-50110d22520a"},"legacyHtmlCaption":"\u003Cp\u003EA network router bears a stern message discouraging the disruption of work.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"},{"__typename":"SlideshowSlide","image":{"__typename":"Image","credit":"Piotr Redlinski for The New York Times","crops":[{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"THREE_BY_TWO","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":126,"name":"thumbWide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W-thumbWide.jpg","width":190}]},{"__typename":"ImageCrop","name":"MASTER","renditions":[{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":683,"name":"jumbo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W-jumbo.jpg","width":1024},{"__typename":"ImageRendition","height":400,"name":"slide","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN-slide-RY5W-slide.jpg","width":600}]}],"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMTQ1ZmM3MjEtNDFlOC01NDhjLWIzY2UtNDA2NGI1NGY4MjNl","summary":"","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F145fc721-41e8-548c-b3ce-4064b54f823e"},"legacyHtmlCaption":"\u003Cp\u003ENeed a trip to the restroom? A glance and a nod between you and your neighbor ensures your computer will still be there when you return.\u003C\u002Fp\u003E"}],"summary":"A Brooklyn cafe with free Internet access is a haven for those who crave silence.","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fslideshow\u002F2010\u002F12\u002F05\u002Fnyregion\u002F05LAPTOPISTAN.html"},"size":"MEDIUM"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":true,"index":16},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"MOST Laptopistanis — Laptopistanites? Laptopistanians? — at Atlas are in their 20s, 30s or early 40s, split evenly between men and women. The dress is casual, with both sexes wearing T-shirts, sweaters and jeans, though a few women seem dressed for “work” with button-down blouses, blazers, even a dress or two. Most Laptopistanis work alone, though occasionally I spotted a group collaborating at a corner table. Socially, Laptopistan is a conservative society; outward displays of emotion are frowned upon. Most people hide behind their screens."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":17},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Aaron Tugendhaft is the exception. He appears at Atlas every morning for a few hours, tie askew, black coffee at his side, some heady-looking book in front of him. Mr. Tugendhaft, who is an adjunct professor of religion at New York University and the editor of a small custom press, is one of the only Atlas regulars I observed sans laptop."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":18},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“I’ve made friends with people because I’m the only guy without a computer,” he told me, quietly, one morning. “A book can be a conversation starter.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":19},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Mr. Tugendhaft has been coming to Atlas nearly every day for three and a half years, but there are many Laptopistanis he has never spoken to. (“Some of them are in the room right now,” he confided in a low voice, eyeing a woman in a jean jacket two tables over.) He has dated fellow Laptopistanis, but not anymore, preferring to keep romance out of the workplace. People tend to keep to themselves, he said, until something breaks the routine: an argument between lovers, news of a subway breakdown, or, most often, some sort of interaction around the power strips."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":20},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“Power is power,” Mr. Tugendhaft said."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Mallory Roberts, a longhaired freelance astrophysicist, said that he had met two girlfriends (now exes), and his current roommate, via power-strip negotiations. “Three months ago Courtney was looking for a plug and we got to talking,” he said of the roommate, with whom he is now working on a documentary about a giant telescope in Puerto Rico. “It turns out we went to the same university, and she needed a place to stay."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":21},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“There’s a recognition here,” he added, “that people come to a cafe to not be alone.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"THE cost of living in Laptopistan, at least for my personal intake of tea, bagels, sandwiches and salad, averaged $12 a day. Other cafes popular with freelancers charge for wireless Internet access and even table time, but the owners of the Atlas, Enrico Lorenzetti and Luca Tesconi, refuse to. Observing the gross daily consumption in Laptopistan, where people seem to nurse a cup of coffee and a cookie for hours, I could not imagine how the two managed to stay in business. But they said the laptops were a stealth economic engine."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":22},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"While the people behind the screens spent a paltry $6 to $10 per day, their true value is as a draw for more profitable takeout customers, Mr. Lorenzetti said. From the moment the door opens at 7 a.m. until it closes at 9 p.m., the place is buzzing, a productive society, visible from the street through wraparound windows. “People come in to buy food and coffee to go, because they see a full crowd,” he said. “They think ‘Hey, this place must be good if I can’t even get a table.’ ”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":23},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"My long-held notion that Laptopistan’s citizens were just sitting around e-mailing other writers in other cafes around the world dissipated as I got to know the MacBook Pro owners around me. Sure, there were aspiring screenwriters, novelists and people updating Twitter, but there was also Gauri Nanda, a product designer from Detroit who created"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","title":"About Clocky.","uri":null,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.nandahome.com\u002Fproducts\u002Fclocky\u002F"}],"text":" Clocky"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":", the alarm clock on wheels that’s featured at the MoMA store and sold worldwide. There was Billy Schultz, a corporate human resources consultant crunching numbers for spreadsheets in PowerPoint and Excel (on a Lenovo PC, no less), and Meredith Sadin, working on her doctorate in American politics at Princeton."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":24},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Laptopistan’s is an entrepreneurial economy, driven by solitary thinkers. "},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","title":"The choreographer’s Web site.","uri":null,"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20240711183700\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.aszurebarton.com\u002F"}],"text":"Aszure Barton"},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":", a choreographer from Alberta, was working with colleagues to prepare for her contemporary dance show called BUSK, which will debut Dec. 17 at the Jerome Robbins Theater. Robert Olinger runs a biotech startup that is getting silkworms to make spider silk at commercial scale, designs online education programs for the New York City Department of Education, and directs theater projects with Russian artists. In just a few days I met architects and event planners, database designers, classical musicians, film editors and app developers, every facet of the creative economy working under one roof, not so much together as in tandem."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":25},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“Here, people have large ambitions,” Mr. Olinger said. “Some have resources, some don’t. They don’t have career plans mapped out, but they have a career in mind. They’re not looking at a particular ladder to climb, they’re looking at a mountain to conquer.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":26},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"WHEN that tree fell that Wednesday afternoon, I was one of the only people who walked over to check it out. I knew I was becoming one of them when a voice in my head said: “O.K., time to get back to work.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":27},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"At home, the slightest change in light is enough of an excuse to get up, walk around, clip my nails or head into the kitchen. Though home offices seem like the perfect work environment, their unrestricted silence, uninterrupted solitude and creature comforts breed distraction. In Laptopistan, I focused with intense precision, sitting motionless for hours at a time. At home, when the Internet goes down, I feel professionally castrated, and usually retire to the sofa until the connection is restored. When the wireless network at Atlas seized up two afternoons in a row, I just switched to off-line tasks without breaking stride. No one else seemed fazed, either. If anything, the place got more crowded. Laptopistan provides structure, and freelancers, like children, secretly crave structure. You come to work, for two or four or eight hours, and you take comfort in the knowledge that everyone else is there to work as well. There’s a silent social pressure to it all."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":28},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"“It almost sounds sick,” said Selena Ross, a fellow Canadian freelance journalist I sat next to one day, “but the fact that people are watching me do my work helps me be what they expect me to be. It’s like working exhibitionism.”"}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":true,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":29},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"Back home in Toronto, with my ergonomically correct chair, spacious desk and dedicated Internet connection, I pulled up my notes from the journey to Laptopistan and tried to write. Within 10 minutes, I was lost in Facebook and watching old “Soul Train” clips on YouTube."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"bad":false,"index":30},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"So I unplugged my laptop, traded my sweat pants for jeans and walked two blocks to the nearest coffee shop. There was some country music playing at a comfortable volume, and the familiar sight of cords along the floor. I took a seat between a guy working on an identical MacBook Pro and a woman drawing in a journal, and I worked like I was back in Atlas: productively, contentedly, fueled by a steady diet of Earl Grey tea, an economically acceptable quantity of cookies, and that social pressure I was craving."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":31},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"After an hour or so, the guy next to me got up to go to the washroom, turned toward his laptop and looked at me."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktop":false,"adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"bad":false,"index":32},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"With a nod, I completed the transaction. When he walked away, I smiled. It was not a big smile; most likely it was barely visible to anyone looking beyond my screen. Precisely the secret smile that citizens of Laptopistan allow themselves when no one’s looking."}],"textAlign":"DEFAULT"}]},"storyFormat":null,"storylines":[{"__typename":"AssociatedStoryline","ruleName":"styln-metro-general","storyline":{"__typename":"Storyline","context":null,"displayName":"Best of Metro","experimentalJsonBlob":"{\"data\":[{\"version\":1,\"type\":\"experimentalJsonBlob\",\"data\":[{\"version\":1,\"type\":\"guide\",\"data\":{\"title\":\"Around the New York Region\",\"leadIn\":\"A look at life, culture, politics and more in New York, New Jersey and Connecticut.\",\"sections\":[{\"section\":[{\"type\":\"bulletedList\",\"value\":[\"\u003Cstrong\u003EManslaughter or Mercy?: \u003C\u002Fstrong\u003EA former doctor is accused of traveling across the United States to help a New York woman asphyxiate herself. In Manhattan, where all-cash purchases shot up to 64% of home sales in April, \u003Ca href=\\\"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2024\u002F07\u002F06\u002Fupshot\u002Fmanhattan-real-estate-cash.html\\\"\u003Eit’s become the standard\u003C\u002Fa\u003E.\",\"\u003Cstrong\u003EStreet Wars: \u003C\u002Fstrong\u003ESoon, dining sheds around New York City will be dismantled — no matter how attractive they are. \u003Ca href=\\\"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2024\u002F07\u002F08\u002Fnyregion\u002Fstreet-wars-dining-sheds-new-york.html\\\"\u003EHere’s why\u003C\u002Fa\u003E.\",\"\u003Cstrong\u003ESunday Routine: \u003C\u002Fstrong\u003EKaitlin Becker, who plays the title role on the children’s show “Meekah,” a spinoff of “Blippi,” \u003Ca href=\\\"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2024\u002F07\u002F06\u002Fnyregion\u002Fkaitlin-becker-meekah-blippi.html\\\"\u003Espends her Sundays\u003C\u002Fa\u003E enjoying movies, fancy treats and museum trips with her 6-year-old son.\"]}]}]}}]}]}","guide":{"__typename":"StorylineGuide","displayName":"Around the New York Region","leadIn":"A look at life, culture, politics and more in New York, New Jersey and Connecticut.","sections":[{"__typename":"StorylineGuideSection","items":[{"__typename":"StorylineGuideItem","content":{"__typename":"ListBlock","content":[{"__typename":"ListItemBlock","content":[{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[{"__typename":"BoldFormat"}],"text":"Manslaughter or Mercy?: "},{"__typename":"TextInline","formats":[],"text":"A former doctor is accused of traveling across the United States to help a New York woman asphyxiate herself.
